facts.statement: Typical observed database workloads reflect a 70/30 read-write ratio, meaning 70% reads and 30% writes on average
Stripe processes over 500 million API requests every day (5,787 requests per second, 347,000 per minute) as of 2025
Netflix scales using stateless microservices deployed across thousands of EC2 instances with horizontal scaling and Chaos Monkey testing for fault injection
Google AlloyDB managed connection pooling boosts client connections by 3x and transactional throughput by up to 5x compared to unmanaged pooling
CAP theorem states that in practice, partition tolerance is mandatory, leaving a choice between consistency and availability when network partitions occur
Vertical scaling has a hard ceiling: you cannot exceed the maximum RAM, CPU, or storage capacity of a single machine
Sharding key cardinality (number of possible values) determines the maximum number of possible shards; a binary field allows only 2 shards
AWS Lambda can scale at over 1,000 concurrent executions per second, adding capacity in milliseconds during traffic spikes
Cold start latency for Lambda functions ranges from 100ms to 2 seconds depending on runtime, code size, and initialization complexity
Consistent hashing reduces key remapping to a subset when nodes are added or removed, unlike traditional hashing which remaps all keys
Cassandra write throughput remains near-constant even under massive concurrency due to its append-only storage engine and distributed commit log
Basic QPS estimation formula: Total Actions per Day ÷ 86,400 seconds (1 day); Peak QPS is typically 2-3x average QPS
PostgreSQL emphasizes full ACID compliance with strong data integrity guarantees, but write overhead limits scale-out capabilities compared to NoSQL systems
Least Connections load balancing outperforms round-robin for long-lived connections (WebSockets, gRPC streams, database sessions lasting seconds or minutes)
IP hash sticky sessions can create hotspots when a single client IP (CDN egress, corporate NAT) represents many users and sends disproportionate traffic
Write-Ahead Log (WAL) buffer tuning reduces disk flushes and improves write performance in ACID databases
Range-based sharding can result in hotspots if data distribution is skewed (e.g., monotonically increasing keys like timestamps)
Redis Pub/Sub enables cache invalidation across distributed nodes without centralized coordination, combining local cache performance with consistency
Serverless functions in Lambda cannot maintain persistent connection pools across invocations; each execution may spin up its own pool, overwhelming databases
Cassandra is designed for horizontal scalability with peer-to-peer architecture; any node can handle requests with automatic sharding and replication across regions

examples.scenario: Millions of simultaneous play requests during peak hours (e.g., Friday evening)
examples.approach: Stateless microservices deployed horizontally across AWS EC2, traffic routed via Zuul API Gateway to healthy instances, Eureka service registry for discovery
examples.outcome: Handles massive concurrent load without single points of failure; Chaos Monkey verifies resilience

examples.scenario: Processing 500M+ API requests daily (5,787 QPS sustained, higher peaks during holiday shopping)
examples.approach: Stateless payment processing service, connection pooling, distributed database replicas, circuit breakers and bulkheading for isolation

examples.scenario: Tweet volume spikes during major events (New Year, sports finals) causing 10x normal traffic in seconds
examples.approach: Horizontal scaling with load balancers, cache-aside pattern for timeline reads, write-heavy database architecture with sharding by user_id
examples.outcome: Service remains responsive; stale reads acceptable during peak load, eventual consistency model

examples.scenario: Billions of daily messages, extremely write-heavy workload
examples.approach: Cassandra for message storage (handles write throughput), Redis for online presence, stateless application servers
examples.outcome: Handles 100+ billion messages/day with <1 second latency

examples.scenario: Vertical scaling ceiling hit: single RDS instance at max r6i.16xlarge (24 CPU, 512 GB RAM) hitting 100% utilization during holiday season
examples.approach: Transitioned to Aurora with read replicas, then to Aurora Serverless, finally Aurora with sharding at application layer
examples.outcome: Unbounded write scaling, but introduced application-level join complexity

examples.scenario: Geographic sharding required: Uber Eats order volume by city varies wildly (NYC 10x San Francisco)
examples.approach: Sharding by city_id to avoid hotspots; separate databases for NYC, SF, LA, etc.; read replicas in each region
examples.outcome: Prevents single database from becoming bottleneck; enables per-region optimization

examples.scenario: Real-time driver location updates: extremely high write rate, time-series data, eventual consistency acceptable
examples.approach: Custom time-series database on Cassandra (write-optimized), Redis for driver-to-rider matching cache
examples.outcome: Handles millions of location updates/second with 100ms+ acceptable staleness

examples.scenario: Cold starts causing 500ms+ latency for user-facing APIs during scale-outs
examples.approach: Initially used Provisioned Concurrency ($400/month for 100 concurrent capacity), then migrated to containers on ECS
examples.outcome: Cut latency by 80%, reduced cost by 60%; containers scale faster for this use case

analogies.analogy: Vertical scaling is like upgrading a single checkout lane in a grocery store with a faster cashier. There's a limit to how fast one person can scan items; horizontal scaling is adding more lanes.
analogies.concept: Single machine scaling limits vs. distributed load distribution

analogies.analogy: Stateless services are like ATMs: each transaction is independent, no machine needs to 'remember' previous interactions. Stateful services are like talking to a bank teller who keeps notes on your account.
analogies.concept: Why stateless services enable true horizontal scaling without session affinity

analogies.analogy: Connection pooling is like a taxi dispatch system reusing cabs instead of calling a new car for every passenger. Serverless functions are like ordering an Uber: a new 'vehicle' spins up for each request.
analogies.concept: Connection overhead in stateless vs. persistent connection management

analogies.analogy: Database sharding is like dividing a library's catalog by first letter: you have separate card catalogs for A-D, E-H, I-L, etc. A bad shard key (like name first letter) creates unbalanced load if 'S' names are common.
analogies.concept: Shard key selection and avoiding hotspots

analogies.analogy: CAP theorem is like a contract: you can have fast delivery or guaranteed accuracy, but not both during a postal strike (network partition). You choose which one to sacrifice.
analogies.concept: Consistency vs. availability tradeoffs under network failures

analogies.analogy: Cache invalidation is like updating a wiki: you need to notify all mirrors when the source changes, or they'll serve stale content. TTL-based expiration is like 'refresh every 5 minutes automatically.'
analogies.concept: Keeping distributed caches consistent at scale

analogies.analogy: A read-heavy database (70% reads) is like a library: most queries are 'what book do you have?' A write-heavy database (70% writes) is like a ledger: most operations record new transactions.
analogies.concept: Database design patterns differ based on read/write ratio

analogies.analogy: Least Connections load balancing is like a smart receptionist who directs incoming calls to whichever colleague has fewest calls on hold, not just round-robin to the next person.
analogies.concept: Why simple algorithms fail for long-lived connections

key_numbers.context: Most web applications are read-heavy; this ratio influences caching and replication strategy
500M daily requests ÷ 86,400 seconds; real company, real load
Peak load typically 2-3x average; use for capacity planning to avoid being understaffed during spikes
Lambda scales in milliseconds; traditional autoscaling scales in minutes
Varies by runtime (Go ~100ms, Python ~500ms), code size, and initialization; impacts user experience

key_numbers.metric: Google AlloyDB connection pooling throughput gain
key_numbers.context: Transaction-mode pooling increases throughput by up to 5x by reusing connections efficiently
Append-only architecture; PostgreSQL write throughput degrades as concurrency increases due to ACID locking
A yes/no shard key limits you to 2 database partitions; poor choice for scaling
Lambda scales faster but with per-request overhead; containers scale slower but amortize startup cost

key_numbers.value: Can create 10:1 load imbalance with CDN egress
key_numbers.context: If sticky sessions route by IP hash, a CDN egress node (1 IP, 1000s of users) overloads one backend

key_numbers.metric: Hash ring key remapping on node addition
key_numbers.context: Consistent hashing; traditional hashing remaps all keys when adding a node

key_numbers.metric: Typical Daily Active Users to QPS
key_numbers.context: Back-of-envelope: 10M actions ÷ 86,400 seconds; actual peak will be 2-3x higher
Guides query planner; buffer_cache should be ~25% of RAM to keep frequently accessed data in memory
Near-instant cache invalidation without central coordinator; acceptable for eventual consistency
Write-heavy workload; requires Cassandra-style append-only architecture, not PostgreSQL
Vertical scaling ceiling for a developer's laptop; can't upgrade beyond built-in limits
Hard ceiling for single-instance relational databases; anything beyond requires sharding or distributed systems
Each connection uses a source port; stateful servers hit this limit with <1M concurrent users

common_misconceptions.misconception: Horizontal scaling solves all performance problems
common_misconceptions.reality: Adding servers helps with CPU and memory bottlenecks, but not database write throughput. A single-instance database bottleneck cannot be solved by adding application servers; you need sharding.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: Engineers add 10 application servers expecting 10x throughput, but database latency remains unchanged, wasting infrastructure budget

common_misconceptions.misconception: Vertical scaling is always cheaper than horizontal scaling
common_misconceptions.reality: Pricing curves differ. A 16-core server costs 3x a 4-core server, not 4x. Distributed systems have coordination overhead. Hybrid approaches often minimize cost.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: Decisions made purely on unit cost ($/GB RAM) ignore licensing, licensing concurrency caps, and complexity cost

common_misconceptions.misconception: Round-robin load balancing works for all workloads
common_misconceptions.reality: Round-robin assumes all requests take equal time. For long-lived connections (WebSockets, database sessions), some servers accumulate hundreds of connections while others are idle. Use Least Connections instead.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: Uneven load distribution causes timeouts and latency spikes on overloaded servers while other servers sit idle

common_misconceptions.misconception: Sticky sessions are always necessary for user experience
common_misconceptions.reality: Sticky sessions hurt scalability. Best practice: move session state to Redis, make services stateless. Session affinity is a last resort.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: Sticky sessions prevent even load distribution, block instance shutdown during deploys, and fail if the user's server crashes

common_misconceptions.misconception: Caching solves everything; use Redis for all data
common_misconceptions.reality: Caching helps reads, not writes. Cache invalidation is hard; stale data risks consistency issues. For heavy writes, you need a write-optimized database, not just cache.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: Engineers over-invest in caching while leaving database writes unoptimized, causing write-throughput bottlenecks

common_misconceptions.reality: Schema still exists; it's just implicit in application code instead of explicit in the database. You must reason about your data model or face inconsistencies.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: Schema-less sounds flexible until application code has to handle 5 different formats of the same field

common_misconceptions.misconception: CAP theorem means you pick one of three; you always have all three
common_misconceptions.reality: You always have partition tolerance (network partitions happen). You choose consistency OR availability when a partition occurs. You can't have both.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: Misunderstanding CAP leads to designs that claim to offer all three, then fail at consistency OR availability under network faults

common_misconceptions.misconception: Connection pooling is a free optimization
common_misconceptions.reality: Connection pooling adds complexity (configuration, lifecycle management). In serverless, connection pools create new overhead per invocation. Sometimes it's the wrong tool.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: Serverless users adding connection pooling accidentally create database connection explosions (1000x increase in connections)

common_misconceptions.misconception: Sharding by user_id prevents all hotspots
common_misconceptions.reality: Sharding key determines load distribution, but some users/entities are inherently hotter (celebrities, VIP accounts). Sharding helps, but doesn't eliminate hotspots.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: A celebrity with 1M followers can cause one shard to handle 100x traffic of others

common_misconceptions.misconception: Serverless is cheaper because you only pay for what you use
common_misconceptions.reality: Serverless has per-request cost and cold start overhead. For sustained load, containers or servers are often cheaper. Cost depends on traffic pattern.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: Migrating a sustained-load workload to Lambda can triple costs despite paying 'per-request'

common_misconceptions.reality: Strong consistency (ACID) requires locking and consensus, reducing write throughput. Eventually-consistent systems (like Cassandra) trade guarantees for throughput.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: Choosing PostgreSQL for a write-heavy workload expecting high throughput leads to lock contention

common_misconceptions.reality: Read replicas scale reads, not writes. Write bottleneck remains on the primary. If your workload is write-heavy, read replicas won't help.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: Adding read replicas without sharding writes leaves write throughput unchanged

common_misconceptions.misconception: Capacity estimation is a one-time calculation
common_misconceptions.reality: Capacity needs grow non-linearly (traffic spikes, holiday seasons, viral features). You must continuously re-estimate and over-provision by 30-50%.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: Estimating once and deploying exact capacity results in cascading failures during the first traffic spike

common_misconceptions.misconception: A database can handle any data type efficiently
common_misconceptions.reality: Different databases optimize for different access patterns. Time-series workloads, graphs, blobs, vectors require specialized storage.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: Storing time-series data in a relational database (one row per metric per timestamp) causes billion-row tables and query timeouts

common_misconceptions.misconception: If you deploy more instances, your system automatically scales
common_misconceptions.reality: Scaling requires stateless services, external session storage, and no shared bottlenecks. Without these, adding instances does nothing.
common_misconceptions.why_matters: Adding instances to a monolith that stores session state in local RAM doesn't scale because each instance is independent
